story:
Brendan’s cabin door shuddered under hard fists. Voices outside, stubborn and angry, declared themselves the King’s men, demanded entry, and threatened to break the door down. Brendan, genial and unhurried—seeming simple, or merely old—bought moments with words and the clink of imagined keys. He turned to Maria, took her by the shoulders, pushed her toward the kitchen, and pressed a large knife into her hand. Run up the hill, through the forest, and don’t look back, he told her. He would talk his way out. If you have to, come back after dark for supplies—then go north and lie low.

She refused. She had already left too much; she would not leave him. Come with me, she urged. No, he answered. These were the King’s men—clever, mounted, relentless. They had tracked her here. If they’d shadowed her closely, she would already be dead or bound—whichever paid better. Run. The pounding grew like thunder, the voices promising to kick the door in and worse out of him. Brendan called back in a quavering tone, playing the pitiable old man, then cracked the back door and pleaded once more. Go. Please go. She cursed under her breath, tears breaking free, and ran with the knife in her hand.

She climbed the pine-clad slope, the ground springy with old brown needles. Her thighs burned. At the crest the forest leveled and she hurried on, thinking of nothing, the trunks at her sides a smear of brown. She realized she was not moving fast at all, yet the world spun. A pain like a slow drill began behind her brow, merciless. A great bang, as if a lid had slammed on a vast urn, rang through her skull. Darkness took her.

Wind in high branches woke her. A small deer, curious, sniffed at her feet, then shot away like an arrow. She stood, shook herself clear, and memory blew through her like a distant war horn. Brendan. She ran back the way she had come—fleet as the deer, then faster, a cheetah over open ground. In moments she burst through the back door.

The kitchen stood empty. She stepped into the living room, and the threshold itself seemed to prick her heart and gut; her forehead throbbed. Brendan lay on the floor, face up, eyes fixed on nothing. Her oldest friend—the man who had saved her life twice before, and now a third time—was gone. She fell to her knees, a scream caught in her hands, and held him. Grief emptied her. She wept and knew nothing else for a time.

When she finally looked up, the room bore signs of strange ruin. A dozen small birds—magpies or blackbirds, she could not tell—lay scorched and scattered, feathers strewn. The front doorway’s frame was burned black. The door itself had vanished. In its place, centered in the threshold, rested a neat gray pile of ash.

She hardly cared—until a hiss rose, like steaming water, like a snake. The ash stirred. It swarmed, not like flies but like a murmuration, spinning and darting, whispering through the room. It hung, eyeless yet somehow watching her, then set to its work, weaving itself into a shape and standing still at last. Words. A message in ash, black and soundless.

She read: Run if you wish, hide if you must—your fate is already cast. Live what moments remain, until the writer chooses to show your soul is no more than dust. When she finished, she lowered her gaze. She made no sound for days—not again until she was far away.
